facts.text: Hartwick Seminary, the oldest Lutheran seminary in the United States, opened in 1812 on land John Christopher Hartwick purchased from the Mohawk Indians in 1763.
The town of Hartwick was carved out of Otsego in 1802, and its northern boundary was shifted a year later.
Hartwick’s original plan was a “New Jerusalem,” but Hartwick’s will redirected his fortune to establish a seminary instead.
The community sits along New York State Route 28, just five miles south of Cooperstown, and the hamlet marks the original site of the seminary now known as Hartwick College.
John Christopher Hartwick, a German Lutheran missionary, bought the 21,500‑acre tract in 1752 for 100 pounds and later left his entire estate to fund the seminary.
The town’s landscape is hilly, with peaks rising 200 to 350 feet above the valleys, and small streams drain into the Susquehanna and Otego creeks.
